import Mathlib

theorem exists_zero_of_interval_integral_eq_zero
    (f : Real → Real) (hf : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1))
    (hint : ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x = 0) :
    ∃ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x = 0 := by
  -- Proof by contradiction using the intermediate value theorem
  by_contra h
  have hne : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x ≠ 0 := by simpa using h
  
  -- Consider the sign of f at 0
  cases' lt_or_gt_of_ne (by rw [hne] <;> simp) with hp0 hn0
  
  case inl => 
    -- f(0) > 0, show contradiction via IVT if there's a point where f(x) < 0
    have hpos : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, 0 < f x := by
      intro x hx
      by_contra hneg
      -- Use intermediate value theorem to find zero
      sorry
    
    -- If f > 0 everywhere, then integral > 0, contradiction
    have hpos_int : 0 < ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x := by
      apply intervalIntegral.integral_pos
      · norm_num
      · exact hf
      · intro x hx; apply hpos; assumption
      · use 0; left; norm_num; exact hp0
    
    contradiction

  case inr => 
    -- f(0) < 0, need to show f < 0 everywhere
    have hneg : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x < 0 := by sorry
    
    -- If f < 0 everywhere, then integral < 0, contradiction  
    have hn_int : ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x < 0 := by
      have hnegf : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, -f x > 0 := by sorry
      sorry
    
    contradiction

import Mathlib

theorem exists_zero_of_interval_integral_eq_zero
    (f : Real → Real) (hf : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1))
    (hint : ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x = 0) :
    ∃ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x = 0 := by
  -- Assume for contradiction that f never vanishes on [0,1]
  by_contra h
  have hne : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x ≠ 0 := by simpa using h
  
  -- Get the sign at a specific point
  obtain ⟨hp0 | hn0⟩ := lt_or_gt_of_ne (hne _ ⟨by norm_num, by norm_num⟩)
  
  case inl => 
    -- f(0) > 0. Since [0,1] is connected and f never vanishes, f must be positive everywhere.
    have hpos : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, 0 < f x := by
      intro x hx
      by_contra hneg
      have hlt : f x < 0 := lt_of_not_ge hneg
      -- Use intermediate value theorem: f goes from positive to negative, so crosses zero
      have hcont : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1) := hf
      have huv : ∃ c ∈ Set.Ioo (0 : Real) 1, f c = 0 := by
        apply intermediate_value_Icc'.2 hf
        norm_num at hx ⊢
        linarith
      contradiction
    -- Integral of positive function is positive
    have hpos_int : 0 < ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x := by
      apply intervalIntegral.integral_pos
      · norm_num
      · exact hf
      · intro x hx; exact hpos x hx
      · use 0; left; norm_num; linarith [hp0]
    contradiction

  case inr => 
    -- f(0) < 0. Similarly, f must be negative everywhere.
    have hneg : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x < 0 := by
      intro x hx
      by_contra hge
      have hgt : f x > 0 := gt_of_lt_neg hge
      have hcont : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1) := hf
      have huv : ∃ c ∈ Set.Ioo (0 : Real) 1, f c = 0 := by
        apply intermediate_value_Icc.2 hf
        norm_num at hx ⊢
        linarith
      contradiction
    -- If f < 0 everywhere, then -f > 0 everywhere, so its integral is positive
    have hneg_int : ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x < 0 := by
      have hpos_negf : ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, -f x > 0 := by
        intro x hx
        rw [show -(-f x) = f x by ring]
        exact fun h => h.ne (hneg x hx)
      have hpos_int_negf : 0 < ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, (-f) x := by
        apply intervalIntegral.integral_pos
        · norm_num
        · exact hf
        · intro x hx; exact hpos_negf x hx
        · use 0; left; norm_num; linarith [hn0]
      -- ∫(-f) = -∫f, so ∫f < 0
      have h : ∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, (-f) x = -(∫ x in (0 : Real)..1, f x) := by
        congr; ext x; ring
      rw [h] at hpos_int_negf
      linarith
    contradiction

  have hsign : (∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, 0 < f x) ∨ (∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (0 : Real) 1, f x < 0) := by

    · exact Or.inl hp

theorem exists_zero_of_interval_integral_eq_zero


**Proof Strategy:** The proof uses contradiction. Assuming f never vanishes on [0,1], since [0,1] is connected and f is continuous, f must maintain constant sign (either always positive or always negative). If f > 0 everywhere, the integral is positive; if f < 0 everywhere, the integral is negative. Both contradict the assumption that the integral equals 0.
